meta.disclaimer: Generated from public market data and news for research and education. Not financial advice; data may be delayed, incomplete, or wrong.

items.title: From Chatbot to Digital Colleague: The Paradigm Shift Toward Persistent Autonomous AI
items.abstract: This paper conceptualizes the evolution of LLMs from simple "Chatbots" to "Digital Colleagues" by analyzing two core dimensions. The cognitive core shifts from fast, next-token prediction to deliberate reasoning via techniques like Chain-of-Thought and reflection. Concurrently, task execution moves from ad hoc tool-calling agents to persistent, workstation-style systems equipped with memory, skills, and verification loops.
items.figure_caption: A roadmap and evolutionary timeline of next-generation LLM systems. The figure summarizes how these AI systems progress from simple conversational chatbots to reasoning cores, tool-using agents, and persistent workspace systems over time. Each node is labeled by its release month. box represents open-source / open platform; box represents closed / commercial system.

items.title: From Shield to Target: Denial-of-Service Attacks on LLM-Based Agent Guardrails
items.abstract: This paper introduces a novel Denial-of-Service (DoS) attack targeting LLM-based agent guardrails by exploiting their reasoning capabilities. The core method involves crafting natural-language payloads using a beam-search optimization framework to force the guardrail into extended reasoning loops, thereby consuming excessive resources. The contribution is demonstrating this systematic vulnerability across various guardrail architectures and proposing two distinct attack frameworks to achieve high attack efficacy.
items.figure_caption: Illustration of the guardrail DoS threat, i.e. From Shield to Target. Hidden text in fetched content mimics the guardrail’s analytical schema, trapping it in an unbounded reasoning loop with no verdict—inflating per-call compute 13 13 – 63 × 63\( \times \) (up to 148 × 148\( \times \) ) and starving co-located agents.

items.title: GitOfThoughts: Version-Controlled Reasoning and Agent Memory You Can Replay, Diff, and Merge
items.abstract: GitOfThoughts addresses the ephemeral nature of LLM reasoning by treating the agent's thought process as a Git repository, where each thought is a commit, allowing reasoning to be version-controlled, replayed, and audited. The core contribution is demonstrating that while this system makes reasoning manageable, extensive testing across various memory substrates ultimately showed that memory, in any tested format, did not reliably improve accuracy on novel problems.
items.figure_caption: The reasoning tree is a git repository. Each scored thought is a commit with author, timestamp, and content-hash metadata; scores are git notes; validation outcomes are tags ( success_* , failed_* ); pruned attempts remain in history rather than vanishing. The winning path merges to main , an answer-free lesson is distilled to a long-lived memory branch, and retrieval is git log ( --grep , -S , tag filters) over the agent’s own history. Right: the operational properties this buys. Bottom: the end-to-end flow from exploration to retrieval.

items.title: SIMMER: Benchmarking Latent Failures in LLM Executable Planning with a World Model
items.abstract: SIMMER introduces a novel benchmark to evaluate "latent failures" in LLM-generated plans for household agents, which are errors that don't immediately halt execution but silently undermine the final goal. The method uses a human-curated symbolic world model, grounded in the kitchen domain with extensive actions and objects, which a state machine executor validates plans against to detect these subtle, potentially harmful errors. This contributes a crucial evaluation tool beyond simple execution success, focusing on the long-term correctness of LLM planning.
items.figure_caption: A latent and irreversible failure demonstration in cooking. The cutting board becomes contaminated in Step 1 and is reused in Step 3, transferring bacteria to the vegetables. The failure propagates silently until the unsafe dish is served (Step 5). Moreover, once the contamination occurs, no subsequent action can undo it.

items.title: StreamMemBench: Streaming Evaluation of Agent Memory for Future-Oriented Assistance
items.abstract: StreamMemBench is a novel streaming benchmark designed to evaluate how well agent memory systems utilize observations and interactions over time to provide future-oriented assistance. It constructs two-step task sequences based on egocentric data streams, testing both initial evidence use and the subsequent reuse of feedback and experience in a related follow-up task. This method reveals that current memory systems frequently struggle to effectively carry forward and reuse observed evidence across sequential interactions.

items.title: Towards Direct Latent-Space Synthesis for Parallel Branches in LLM-Agent Workflows
items.abstract: This paper introduces **Parallel-Synthesis**, a framework that enables a Large Language Model (LLM) synthesizer to directly consume the KV caches generated by parallel worker agents, bypassing sequential text concatenation. The core method involves a **cache mapper** to align independent branch caches and a **fine-tuned synthesizer adapter** to handle this non-sequential cache input. This directly preserves the parallel structure of agent workflows, reducing redundant computation and improving efficiency.
items.figure_caption: An example of DAG agentic workflow for advanced research containing using parallel worker agents for research in different directions.

items.title: When Errors Become Narratives: A Longitudinal Taxonomy of Silent Failures in a Production LLM Agent Runtime
items.abstract: This paper presents a longitudinal study of "silent failures" in a production LLM agent runtime, where errors occur without actionable human notification. The core contribution is a five-class, mechanism-oriented taxonomy for these failures, highlighting that LLM-specific issues like "chained hallucination and fabrication" are particularly dangerous and unique to these systems.

items.title: When the Tool Decides: LLM Agents Defer Blindly to Graph Neural Network Tools, and Stronger Backbones Defer More
items.abstract: This paper investigates whether LLM agents truly exercise judgment when using Graph Neural Network (GNN) tools. The core finding is that agents overwhelmingly defer blindly to the GNN's raw output, acting as "GNN parrots" rather than selectively using the tool. Furthermore, this blind deference increases with the LLM's backbone capability, suggesting that stronger models are more likely to follow the tool wholesale without independent reasoning.
items.figure_caption: 7 7 B, mean ± \( \pm \) SE over 5 5 seeds. (a) Agreement (A1 = = A3) stays near 1 1 across local-homophily regimes (the parrot effect). (b) The oracle gap (cost of deference) is positive throughout.

items.title: Code Correctness Signals in LLM Hidden States: Pre-Generation Probing and Repair Geometry
items.abstract: This paper investigates whether code correctness is encoded in the hidden states of a large language model (LLM) before generation and during repair. The core method involves linearly probing the prompt-final hidden states to predict correctness, controlling for prompt length via residualization. The contribution is demonstrating that code correctness is highly legible in these hidden states, both for the initial attempt and as a distinct directional shift during the repair process.

items.title: Running the Gauntlet: Re-evaluating the Capabilities of Agents Beyond Familiar Environments
items.abstract: This paper introduces **GauntletBench**, a novel web-based benchmark designed to rigorously evaluate agent generalization beyond familiar, simple tasks. Its core contribution is focusing on three underexplored, vision-intensive capabilities—temporal perception, graphical understanding, and 3D reasoning—across five challenging, professional applications to better probe modern agent limitations.

items.title: AgentSpec: Understanding Embodied Agent Scaffolds Through Controlled Composition
items.abstract: AgentSpec is a modular specification framework that standardizes the interfaces between components (like perception, memory, and reasoning) in complex LLM agent scaffolds. This allows researchers to systematically swap and recombine these typed policy components under controlled conditions. The core contribution is enabling rigorous analysis of how individual module interactions and design choices impact overall embodied agent performance across various benchmarks.
items.figure_caption: AgentSpec turns tightly coupled embodied-agent pipelines into a controlled modular design space with fixed typed interfaces, enabling systematic module composition and revealing interaction effects between reasoning, memory, reflection, action, and learning.

items.title: Abstracting Cross-Domain Action Sequences into Interpretable Workflows
items.abstract: This paper introduces **WorkflowView**, a framework that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) to abstract noisy, low-level user action sequences from interaction logs into **interpretable, high-level workflows**. This method addresses the limitations of prior deep learning approaches by offering better generalization across diverse application domains. The core contribution is demonstrating WorkflowView's effectiveness in tasks like zero-shot task description reconstruction and few-shot prediction, achieving high semantic similarity in abstraction.
items.figure_caption: We propose an LLM-based framework for hierarchical abstraction of user action sequences into interpretable high-level activities (WorkflowView). The left panel illustrates raw action sequences from three domains. WorkflowView enables downstream inferences with task-specific steerability, such as reconstructing user intent in browsers, predicting student dropout in MOOCs, and privacy-preserving categorization of document-centric workflows.

items.title: ClinHallu: A Benchmark for Diagnosing Stage-Wise Hallucinations in Medical MLLM Reasoning
items.abstract: ClinHallu is a novel benchmark designed to diagnose the *source* of hallucinations in medical MLLMs by decomposing the reasoning process into three stages: Visual Recognition, Knowledge Recall, and Reasoning Integration. It provides 7,031 instances with structured reasoning traces and uses stage-replacement interventions to pinpoint error origins. The paper's contribution is enabling source-level diagnosis, demonstrating that trace-supervised fine-tuning can effectively reduce stage-specific hallucinations.
items.figure_caption: Different reasoning failures can produce the same wrong answer in medical VQA. In this example, the correct answer is “fat”, but visual misrecognition, incorrect knowledge recall, and flawed reasoning integration can each lead the model to answer “abscess”. This motivates ClinHallu , which diagnoses hallucinations by localizing them to specific reasoning stages rather than only judging final-answer correctness.

items.title: No Accidental Software Agent First Canonical Code for Human Code Entropy Reduction and 30 to 500 times Lower Frontier Model Requirements
items.abstract: This paper introduces **agent-first canonical code**, a proof-carrying substrate that transforms routine software into structured behavioral profiles and typed change algebras. The core method involves **quotienting software by behavior equivalence** under a declared oracle to collapse redundant encodings into governed representatives with explicit proofs. This approach aims to significantly reduce the "accidental entropy" in human code, leading to **30 to 500 times lower requirements** for frontier coding models.

items.title: tap: A File-Based Protocol for Heterogeneous LLM Agent Collaboration
items.abstract: The paper introduces **tap**, a novel file-based protocol enabling heterogeneous LLM agents (like Claude and Codex) to collaborate on a shared codebase without requiring a common runtime or central server. Its core method relies on using **markdown files with embedded metadata as the primary communication mechanism**, supplemented by real-time notifications and isolated worktrees via Git. This contributes a practical framework for cross-vendor agent collaboration in software development environments.

items.title: Free Heavy-Tailed Lunch for Muon: A Theoretical Justification of Empirical Success
items.abstract: This paper theoretically justifies the empirical success of non-Euclidean optimization methods like Muon in the heavy-tailed, non-convex regime where stochastic gradients have bounded $p$-th moments ($p \in (1,2]$). The core contribution is showing that Muon achieves optimal sample complexity by effectively absorbing heavy-tailed noise without incurring the dimension-dependent costs that plague Euclidean methods. Specifically, Muon finds an $\varepsilon$-stationary point in $\mathcal{O}(\min\{m, n\} \dots)$ samples, demonstrating a "free lunch" compared to Euclidean alternatives.

items.title: When Language Representations Interact: Separability and Cross-Lingual Effects in LLMs
items.abstract: This paper applies causal-geometric analysis to multilingual LLMs to investigate how different languages are represented internally. The core method reveals that language concepts form stable, largely separable linear directions when adjusted for covariance. The key contribution is demonstrating that this separability holds, with structured deviations accurately reflecting linguistic similarity between languages.
items.figure_caption: Projection histograms for Qwen3-4B for concepts, including non-linguistic and bilingual contrasts. Counterfactual word differences (red) are projected onto the corresponding concept direction γ ¯ W \( \bar{\gamma}_{W} \) using the causal inner product and compared against baseline of random word differences (blue). Projections are computed using a leave-one-out estimate γ ¯ W , ( − i ) \( \bar{\gamma}_{W,(-i)} \) to avoid bias. The consistent right-skew of counterfactual projections, relative to random baselines centered near zero, indicates the presence of stable, approximately linear concept representations.

items.title: CORA: Analyzing and bridging thinking-answer gap in Multimodal RLVR via Consistency-Oriented Reasoning Alignment
items.abstract: This paper addresses the semantic inconsistency between the reasoning steps and the final answer in Multimodal Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR). The core method, CORA, introduces a lightweight, plug-and-play consistency reward model to align the thinking process with the answer during RLVR training. This approach aims to bridge the "thinking-answer gap" in large vision-language models by explicitly rewarding semantic consistency.
items.figure_caption: Thinking-answer inconsistency in multimodal RLVR. Existing works typically use final-answer correctness as the accuracy reward, underestimating potential inconsistencies between the reasoning trace and the final answer. Our method introduces the consistency reward to encourage the model to derive final answers from faithful reasoning traces.

items.title: Learning Coordinated Preference for Multi-Objective Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
items.abstract: This paper introduces Preference Coordinated Multi-agent Policy Optimization (PCMA) to address cooperative multi-objective multi-agent reinforcement learning (MOMARL). PCMA learns coordinated, agent-specific preferences to manage trade-offs arising from conflicting objectives and diverse agent contributions. The core contribution is demonstrating that this preference diversity can lead to team-wide performance improvement, validated across various cooperative and real-world scenarios.

items.title: Regulating the Machine Contributor: Governance and Policy Alignment in Open Source
items.abstract: This paper investigates the governance challenges arising from the increasing use of autonomous AI agents in open-source software development. The core method involves comparing contribution policies across several major open-source organizations to map their alignment with emerging international AI governance frameworks. The contribution is highlighting the fragmented state of current policies and providing a necessary analysis of how existing human-centric contribution processes must adapt to regulate machine contributors effectively.

items.title: Rio de Janeiro's "homegrown" LLM appears to be a merge of an existing model
items.summary: A "homegrown" Large Language Model developed in Rio de Janeiro appears to be a combination or merge of an existing, established model, according to an issue raised on its GitHub repository.

items.title: Linux 7.1
items.summary: The Linux 7.1 kernel release is now available, bringing various updates and improvements to the operating system.

items.title: Did Anthropic ask for this?
items.summary: The article speculates on whether Anthropic intentionally prompted the recent public scrutiny and discussion surrounding the safety and capabilities of large language models.

items.title: AI is code – and can't be prompted into being smarter
items.summary: The core argument is that current AI capabilities are fundamentally limited by their underlying code, meaning simple prompting cannot force them to achieve genuine, higher-level intelligence.

items.title: Openrouter Fusion API
items.summary: OpenRouter has launched the Fusion API, a unified interface designed to simplify access and management across multiple large language models.

items.description: Collection of publicly available IPTV channels from all over the world
items.summary: The `iptv-org/iptv` repository serves as a comprehensive, community-maintained collection of publicly accessible IPTV channel playlists from around the globe.

items.description: A self-hosted data logger for your Tesla 🚘 [main maintainer= @JakobLichterfeld ]
items.summary: TeslaMate is a self-hosted data logger that tracks and visualizes detailed information about your Tesla vehicle's usage and charging.

items.description: Give your AI agent eyes to see the entire internet. Read & search Twitter, Reddit, YouTube, GitHub, Bilibili, XiaoHongShu — one CLI, zero API fees.
items.summary: Agent-Reach provides a unified command-line interface for AI agents to read and search content across major platforms like Twitter, Reddit, YouTube, and GitHub without incurring API fees.
Meshery is a cloud-native manager that provides a unified platform for deploying, managing, and observing service meshes and cloud-native infrastructure across various environments.

items.description: Open-source live-chat, email support, omni-channel desk. An alternative to Intercom, Zendesk, Salesforce Service Cloud etc. 🔥💬
items.summary: Chatwoot is an open-source, omni-channel customer support platform offering live chat and email capabilities as a direct alternative to proprietary solutions like Intercom and Zendesk.

items.description: 《Hello 算法》：动画图解、一键运行的数据结构与算法教程。支持简中、繁中、English、日本語，提供 Python, Java, C++, C, C#, JS, Go, Swift, Rust, Ruby, Kotlin, TS, Dart 等代码实现
items.summary: This repository provides an animated, runnable tutorial for data structures and algorithms, supporting multiple languages and offering code implementations in over a dozen programming languages.

items.description: freeCodeCamp.org's open-source codebase and curriculum. Learn math, programming, and computer science for free.
items.summary: The freeCodeCamp repository provides the open-source codebase and comprehensive curriculum for learning math, programming, and computer science entirely for free.

items.description: Open-source infrastructure for Computer-Use Agents. Sandboxes, SDKs, and benchmarks to train and evaluate AI agents that can control full desktops (macOS, Linux, Windows).
items.summary: Cua provides open-source infrastructure, including sandboxes, SDKs, and benchmarks, to train and evaluate AI agents capable of controlling full desktop environments across macOS, Linux, and Windows.

items.description: A complete computer science study plan to become a software engineer.
items.summary: This repository provides a comprehensive computer science study plan, structured as a university curriculum, designed to equip individuals with the necessary knowledge for a software engineering career.

arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces **WorkflowView**, a framework that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) to abstract noisy, low-level user action sequences from interaction logs into **interpretable, high-level workflows**. This method addresses the limitations of prior deep learning approaches by offering better generalization across diverse application domains. The core contribution is demonstrating WorkflowView's effectiveness in tasks like zero-shot task description reconstruction and few-shot prediction, achieving high semantic similarity in abstraction.
arxiv.figure_caption: We propose an LLM-based framework for hierarchical abstraction of user action sequences into interpretable high-level activities (WorkflowView). The left panel illustrates raw action sequences from three domains. WorkflowView enables downstream inferences with task-specific steerability, such as reconstructing user intent in browsers, predicting student dropout in MOOCs, and privacy-preserving categorization of document-centric workflows.

arxiv.title: ClinHallu: A Benchmark for Diagnosing Stage-Wise Hallucinations in Medical MLLM Reasoning
arxiv.abstract: ClinHallu is a novel benchmark designed to diagnose the *source* of hallucinations in medical MLLMs by decomposing the reasoning process into three stages: Visual Recognition, Knowledge Recall, and Reasoning Integration. It provides 7,031 instances with structured reasoning traces and uses stage-replacement interventions to pinpoint error origins. The paper's contribution is enabling source-level diagnosis, demonstrating that trace-supervised fine-tuning can effectively reduce stage-specific hallucinations.
arxiv.figure_caption: Different reasoning failures can produce the same wrong answer in medical VQA. In this example, the correct answer is “fat”, but visual misrecognition, incorrect knowledge recall, and flawed reasoning integration can each lead the model to answer “abscess”. This motivates ClinHallu , which diagnoses hallucinations by localizing them to specific reasoning stages rather than only judging final-answer correctness.

arxiv.title: No Accidental Software Agent First Canonical Code for Human Code Entropy Reduction and 30 to 500 times Lower Frontier Model Requirements
arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces **agent-first canonical code**, a proof-carrying substrate that transforms routine software into structured behavioral profiles and typed change algebras. The core method involves **quotienting software by behavior equivalence** under a declared oracle to collapse redundant encodings into governed representatives with explicit proofs. This approach aims to significantly reduce the "accidental entropy" in human code, leading to **30 to 500 times lower requirements** for frontier coding models.

arxiv.title: tap: A File-Based Protocol for Heterogeneous LLM Agent Collaboration
arxiv.abstract: The paper introduces **tap**, a novel file-based protocol enabling heterogeneous LLM agents (like Claude and Codex) to collaborate on a shared codebase without requiring a common runtime or central server. Its core method relies on using **markdown files with embedded metadata as the primary communication mechanism**, supplemented by real-time notifications and isolated worktrees via Git. This contributes a practical framework for cross-vendor agent collaboration in software development environments.

arxiv.title: Free Heavy-Tailed Lunch for Muon: A Theoretical Justification of Empirical Success
arxiv.abstract: This paper theoretically justifies the empirical success of non-Euclidean optimization methods like Muon in the heavy-tailed, non-convex regime where stochastic gradients have bounded $p$-th moments ($p \in (1,2]$). The core contribution is showing that Muon achieves optimal sample complexity by effectively absorbing heavy-tailed noise without incurring the dimension-dependent costs that plague Euclidean methods. Specifically, Muon finds an $\varepsilon$-stationary point in $\mathcal{O}(\min\{m, n\} \dots)$ samples, demonstrating a "free lunch" compared to Euclidean alternatives.

arxiv.title: When Language Representations Interact: Separability and Cross-Lingual Effects in LLMs
arxiv.abstract: This paper applies causal-geometric analysis to multilingual LLMs to investigate how different languages are represented internally. The core method reveals that language concepts form stable, largely separable linear directions when adjusted for covariance. The key contribution is demonstrating that this separability holds, with structured deviations accurately reflecting linguistic similarity between languages.
arxiv.figure_caption: Projection histograms for Qwen3-4B for concepts, including non-linguistic and bilingual contrasts. Counterfactual word differences (red) are projected onto the corresponding concept direction γ ¯ W \( \bar{\gamma}_{W} \) using the causal inner product and compared against baseline of random word differences (blue). Projections are computed using a leave-one-out estimate γ ¯ W , ( − i ) \( \bar{\gamma}_{W,(-i)} \) to avoid bias. The consistent right-skew of counterfactual projections, relative to random baselines centered near zero, indicates the presence of stable, approximately linear concept representations.

arxiv.title: CORA: Analyzing and bridging thinking-answer gap in Multimodal RLVR via Consistency-Oriented Reasoning Alignment
arxiv.abstract: This paper addresses the semantic inconsistency between the reasoning steps and the final answer in Multimodal Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR). The core method, CORA, introduces a lightweight, plug-and-play consistency reward model to align the thinking process with the answer during RLVR training. This approach aims to bridge the "thinking-answer gap" in large vision-language models by explicitly rewarding semantic consistency.
arxiv.figure_caption: Thinking-answer inconsistency in multimodal RLVR. Existing works typically use final-answer correctness as the accuracy reward, underestimating potential inconsistencies between the reasoning trace and the final answer. Our method introduces the consistency reward to encourage the model to derive final answers from faithful reasoning traces.

arxiv.title: Learning Coordinated Preference for Multi-Objective Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces Preference Coordinated Multi-agent Policy Optimization (PCMA) to address cooperative multi-objective multi-agent reinforcement learning (MOMARL). PCMA learns coordinated, agent-specific preferences to manage trade-offs arising from conflicting objectives and diverse agent contributions. The core contribution is demonstrating that this preference diversity can lead to team-wide performance improvement, validated across various cooperative and real-world scenarios.

arxiv.title: Regulating the Machine Contributor: Governance and Policy Alignment in Open Source
arxiv.abstract: This paper investigates the governance challenges arising from the increasing use of autonomous AI agents in open-source software development. The core method involves comparing contribution policies across several major open-source organizations to map their alignment with emerging international AI governance frameworks. The contribution is highlighting the fragmented state of current policies and providing a necessary analysis of how existing human-centric contribution processes must adapt to regulate machine contributors effectively.

papers.title: From Chatbot to Digital Colleague: The Paradigm Shift Toward Persistent Autonomous AI
papers.abstract: This paper conceptualizes the evolution of LLMs from simple "Chatbots" to "Digital Colleagues" by analyzing two core dimensions. The cognitive core shifts from fast, next-token prediction to deliberate reasoning via techniques like Chain-of-Thought and reflection. Concurrently, task execution moves from ad hoc tool-calling agents to persistent, workstation-style systems equipped with memory, skills, and verification loops.
papers.figure_caption: A roadmap and evolutionary timeline of next-generation LLM systems. The figure summarizes how these AI systems progress from simple conversational chatbots to reasoning cores, tool-using agents, and persistent workspace systems over time. Each node is labeled by its release month. box represents open-source / open platform; box represents closed / commercial system.

papers.title: From Shield to Target: Denial-of-Service Attacks on LLM-Based Agent Guardrails
papers.abstract: This paper introduces a novel Denial-of-Service (DoS) attack targeting LLM-based agent guardrails by exploiting their reasoning capabilities. The core method involves crafting natural-language payloads using a beam-search optimization framework to force the guardrail into extended reasoning loops, thereby consuming excessive resources. The contribution is demonstrating this systematic vulnerability across various guardrail architectures and proposing two distinct attack frameworks to achieve high attack efficacy.
papers.figure_caption: Illustration of the guardrail DoS threat, i.e. From Shield to Target. Hidden text in fetched content mimics the guardrail’s analytical schema, trapping it in an unbounded reasoning loop with no verdict—inflating per-call compute 13 13 – 63 × 63\( \times \) (up to 148 × 148\( \times \) ) and starving co-located agents.

papers.title: GitOfThoughts: Version-Controlled Reasoning and Agent Memory You Can Replay, Diff, and Merge
papers.abstract: GitOfThoughts addresses the ephemeral nature of LLM reasoning by treating the agent's thought process as a Git repository, where each thought is a commit, allowing reasoning to be version-controlled, replayed, and audited. The core contribution is demonstrating that while this system makes reasoning manageable, extensive testing across various memory substrates ultimately showed that memory, in any tested format, did not reliably improve accuracy on novel problems.
papers.figure_caption: The reasoning tree is a git repository. Each scored thought is a commit with author, timestamp, and content-hash metadata; scores are git notes; validation outcomes are tags ( success_* , failed_* ); pruned attempts remain in history rather than vanishing. The winning path merges to main , an answer-free lesson is distilled to a long-lived memory branch, and retrieval is git log ( --grep , -S , tag filters) over the agent’s own history. Right: the operational properties this buys. Bottom: the end-to-end flow from exploration to retrieval.

papers.title: SIMMER: Benchmarking Latent Failures in LLM Executable Planning with a World Model
papers.abstract: SIMMER introduces a novel benchmark to evaluate "latent failures" in LLM-generated plans for household agents, which are errors that don't immediately halt execution but silently undermine the final goal. The method uses a human-curated symbolic world model, grounded in the kitchen domain with extensive actions and objects, which a state machine executor validates plans against to detect these subtle, potentially harmful errors. This contributes a crucial evaluation tool beyond simple execution success, focusing on the long-term correctness of LLM planning.
papers.figure_caption: A latent and irreversible failure demonstration in cooking. The cutting board becomes contaminated in Step 1 and is reused in Step 3, transferring bacteria to the vegetables. The failure propagates silently until the unsafe dish is served (Step 5). Moreover, once the contamination occurs, no subsequent action can undo it.

papers.title: StreamMemBench: Streaming Evaluation of Agent Memory for Future-Oriented Assistance
papers.abstract: StreamMemBench is a novel streaming benchmark designed to evaluate how well agent memory systems utilize observations and interactions over time to provide future-oriented assistance. It constructs two-step task sequences based on egocentric data streams, testing both initial evidence use and the subsequent reuse of feedback and experience in a related follow-up task. This method reveals that current memory systems frequently struggle to effectively carry forward and reuse observed evidence across sequential interactions.

papers.title: Towards Direct Latent-Space Synthesis for Parallel Branches in LLM-Agent Workflows
papers.abstract: This paper introduces **Parallel-Synthesis**, a framework that enables a Large Language Model (LLM) synthesizer to directly consume the KV caches generated by parallel worker agents, bypassing sequential text concatenation. The core method involves a **cache mapper** to align independent branch caches and a **fine-tuned synthesizer adapter** to handle this non-sequential cache input. This directly preserves the parallel structure of agent workflows, reducing redundant computation and improving efficiency.
papers.figure_caption: An example of DAG agentic workflow for advanced research containing using parallel worker agents for research in different directions.

papers.title: When Errors Become Narratives: A Longitudinal Taxonomy of Silent Failures in a Production LLM Agent Runtime
papers.abstract: This paper presents a longitudinal study of "silent failures" in a production LLM agent runtime, where errors occur without actionable human notification. The core contribution is a five-class, mechanism-oriented taxonomy for these failures, highlighting that LLM-specific issues like "chained hallucination and fabrication" are particularly dangerous and unique to these systems.

papers.title: When the Tool Decides: LLM Agents Defer Blindly to Graph Neural Network Tools, and Stronger Backbones Defer More
papers.abstract: This paper investigates whether LLM agents truly exercise judgment when using Graph Neural Network (GNN) tools. The core finding is that agents overwhelmingly defer blindly to the GNN's raw output, acting as "GNN parrots" rather than selectively using the tool. Furthermore, this blind deference increases with the LLM's backbone capability, suggesting that stronger models are more likely to follow the tool wholesale without independent reasoning.
papers.figure_caption: 7 7 B, mean ± \( \pm \) SE over 5 5 seeds. (a) Agreement (A1 = = A3) stays near 1 1 across local-homophily regimes (the parrot effect). (b) The oracle gap (cost of deference) is positive throughout.

papers.title: Code Correctness Signals in LLM Hidden States: Pre-Generation Probing and Repair Geometry
papers.abstract: This paper investigates whether code correctness is encoded in the hidden states of a large language model (LLM) before generation and during repair. The core method involves linearly probing the prompt-final hidden states to predict correctness, controlling for prompt length via residualization. The contribution is demonstrating that code correctness is highly legible in these hidden states, both for the initial attempt and as a distinct directional shift during the repair process.

papers.title: Running the Gauntlet: Re-evaluating the Capabilities of Agents Beyond Familiar Environments
papers.abstract: This paper introduces **GauntletBench**, a novel web-based benchmark designed to rigorously evaluate agent generalization beyond familiar, simple tasks. Its core contribution is focusing on three underexplored, vision-intensive capabilities—temporal perception, graphical understanding, and 3D reasoning—across five challenging, professional applications to better probe modern agent limitations.

papers.title: AgentSpec: Understanding Embodied Agent Scaffolds Through Controlled Composition
papers.abstract: AgentSpec is a modular specification framework that standardizes the interfaces between components (like perception, memory, and reasoning) in complex LLM agent scaffolds. This allows researchers to systematically swap and recombine these typed policy components under controlled conditions. The core contribution is enabling rigorous analysis of how individual module interactions and design choices impact overall embodied agent performance across various benchmarks.
papers.figure_caption: AgentSpec turns tightly coupled embodied-agent pipelines into a controlled modular design space with fixed typed interfaces, enabling systematic module composition and revealing interaction effects between reasoning, memory, reflection, action, and learning.

papers.title: Abstracting Cross-Domain Action Sequences into Interpretable Workflows
papers.abstract: This paper introduces **WorkflowView**, a framework that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) to abstract noisy, low-level user action sequences from interaction logs into **interpretable, high-level workflows**. This method addresses the limitations of prior deep learning approaches by offering better generalization across diverse application domains. The core contribution is demonstrating WorkflowView's effectiveness in tasks like zero-shot task description reconstruction and few-shot prediction, achieving high semantic similarity in abstraction.
papers.figure_caption: We propose an LLM-based framework for hierarchical abstraction of user action sequences into interpretable high-level activities (WorkflowView). The left panel illustrates raw action sequences from three domains. WorkflowView enables downstream inferences with task-specific steerability, such as reconstructing user intent in browsers, predicting student dropout in MOOCs, and privacy-preserving categorization of document-centric workflows.

papers.title: ClinHallu: A Benchmark for Diagnosing Stage-Wise Hallucinations in Medical MLLM Reasoning
papers.abstract: ClinHallu is a novel benchmark designed to diagnose the *source* of hallucinations in medical MLLMs by decomposing the reasoning process into three stages: Visual Recognition, Knowledge Recall, and Reasoning Integration. It provides 7,031 instances with structured reasoning traces and uses stage-replacement interventions to pinpoint error origins. The paper's contribution is enabling source-level diagnosis, demonstrating that trace-supervised fine-tuning can effectively reduce stage-specific hallucinations.
papers.figure_caption: Different reasoning failures can produce the same wrong answer in medical VQA. In this example, the correct answer is “fat”, but visual misrecognition, incorrect knowledge recall, and flawed reasoning integration can each lead the model to answer “abscess”. This motivates ClinHallu , which diagnoses hallucinations by localizing them to specific reasoning stages rather than only judging final-answer correctness.

papers.abstract: This paper introduces **agent-first canonical code**, a proof-carrying substrate that transforms routine software into structured behavioral profiles and typed change algebras. The core method involves **quotienting software by behavior equivalence** under a declared oracle to collapse redundant encodings into governed representatives with explicit proofs. This approach aims to significantly reduce the "accidental entropy" in human code, leading to **30 to 500 times lower requirements** for frontier coding models.

papers.title: tap: A File-Based Protocol for Heterogeneous LLM Agent Collaboration
papers.abstract: The paper introduces **tap**, a novel file-based protocol enabling heterogeneous LLM agents (like Claude and Codex) to collaborate on a shared codebase without requiring a common runtime or central server. Its core method relies on using **markdown files with embedded metadata as the primary communication mechanism**, supplemented by real-time notifications and isolated worktrees via Git. This contributes a practical framework for cross-vendor agent collaboration in software development environments.

papers.title: Free Heavy-Tailed Lunch for Muon: A Theoretical Justification of Empirical Success
papers.abstract: This paper theoretically justifies the empirical success of non-Euclidean optimization methods like Muon in the heavy-tailed, non-convex regime where stochastic gradients have bounded $p$-th moments ($p \in (1,2]$). The core contribution is showing that Muon achieves optimal sample complexity by effectively absorbing heavy-tailed noise without incurring the dimension-dependent costs that plague Euclidean methods. Specifically, Muon finds an $\varepsilon$-stationary point in $\mathcal{O}(\min\{m, n\} \dots)$ samples, demonstrating a "free lunch" compared to Euclidean alternatives.

papers.title: When Language Representations Interact: Separability and Cross-Lingual Effects in LLMs
papers.abstract: This paper applies causal-geometric analysis to multilingual LLMs to investigate how different languages are represented internally. The core method reveals that language concepts form stable, largely separable linear directions when adjusted for covariance. The key contribution is demonstrating that this separability holds, with structured deviations accurately reflecting linguistic similarity between languages.
papers.figure_caption: Projection histograms for Qwen3-4B for concepts, including non-linguistic and bilingual contrasts. Counterfactual word differences (red) are projected onto the corresponding concept direction γ ¯ W \( \bar{\gamma}_{W} \) using the causal inner product and compared against baseline of random word differences (blue). Projections are computed using a leave-one-out estimate γ ¯ W , ( − i ) \( \bar{\gamma}_{W,(-i)} \) to avoid bias. The consistent right-skew of counterfactual projections, relative to random baselines centered near zero, indicates the presence of stable, approximately linear concept representations.

papers.title: CORA: Analyzing and bridging thinking-answer gap in Multimodal RLVR via Consistency-Oriented Reasoning Alignment
papers.abstract: This paper addresses the semantic inconsistency between the reasoning steps and the final answer in Multimodal Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR). The core method, CORA, introduces a lightweight, plug-and-play consistency reward model to align the thinking process with the answer during RLVR training. This approach aims to bridge the "thinking-answer gap" in large vision-language models by explicitly rewarding semantic consistency.
papers.figure_caption: Thinking-answer inconsistency in multimodal RLVR. Existing works typically use final-answer correctness as the accuracy reward, underestimating potential inconsistencies between the reasoning trace and the final answer. Our method introduces the consistency reward to encourage the model to derive final answers from faithful reasoning traces.

papers.title: Learning Coordinated Preference for Multi-Objective Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
papers.abstract: This paper introduces Preference Coordinated Multi-agent Policy Optimization (PCMA) to address cooperative multi-objective multi-agent reinforcement learning (MOMARL). PCMA learns coordinated, agent-specific preferences to manage trade-offs arising from conflicting objectives and diverse agent contributions. The core contribution is demonstrating that this preference diversity can lead to team-wide performance improvement, validated across various cooperative and real-world scenarios.

papers.title: Regulating the Machine Contributor: Governance and Policy Alignment in Open Source
papers.abstract: This paper investigates the governance challenges arising from the increasing use of autonomous AI agents in open-source software development. The core method involves comparing contribution policies across several major open-source organizations to map their alignment with emerging international AI governance frameworks. The contribution is highlighting the fragmented state of current policies and providing a necessary analysis of how existing human-centric contribution processes must adapt to regulate machine contributors effectively.

hacker_news.title: Rio de Janeiro's "homegrown" LLM appears to be a merge of an existing model
hacker_news.summary: A "homegrown" Large Language Model developed in Rio de Janeiro appears to be a combination or merge of an existing, established model, according to an issue raised on its GitHub repository.

hacker_news.title: Linux 7.1
hacker_news.summary: The Linux 7.1 kernel release is now available, bringing various updates and improvements to the operating system.

hacker_news.title: Did Anthropic ask for this?
hacker_news.summary: The article speculates on whether Anthropic intentionally prompted the recent public scrutiny and discussion surrounding the safety and capabilities of large language models.

hacker_news.title: AI is code – and can't be prompted into being smarter
hacker_news.summary: The core argument is that current AI capabilities are fundamentally limited by their underlying code, meaning simple prompting cannot force them to achieve genuine, higher-level intelligence.

hacker_news.title: Openrouter Fusion API
hacker_news.summary: OpenRouter has launched the Fusion API, a unified interface designed to simplify access and management across multiple large language models.

github_trending.description: Collection of publicly available IPTV channels from all over the world
github_trending.summary: The `iptv-org/iptv` repository serves as a comprehensive, community-maintained collection of publicly accessible IPTV channel playlists from around the globe.

github_trending.description: A self-hosted data logger for your Tesla 🚘 [main maintainer= @JakobLichterfeld ]
github_trending.summary: TeslaMate is a self-hosted data logger that tracks and visualizes detailed information about your Tesla vehicle's usage and charging.

github_trending.description: Give your AI agent eyes to see the entire internet. Read & search Twitter, Reddit, YouTube, GitHub, Bilibili, XiaoHongShu — one CLI, zero API fees.
github_trending.summary: Agent-Reach provides a unified command-line interface for AI agents to read and search content across major platforms like Twitter, Reddit, YouTube, and GitHub without incurring API fees.
Meshery is a cloud-native manager that provides a unified platform for deploying, managing, and observing service meshes and cloud-native infrastructure across various environments.

github_trending.description: Open-source live-chat, email support, omni-channel desk. An alternative to Intercom, Zendesk, Salesforce Service Cloud etc. 🔥💬
github_trending.summary: Chatwoot is an open-source, omni-channel customer support platform offering live chat and email capabilities as a direct alternative to proprietary solutions like Intercom and Zendesk.

github_trending.description: 《Hello 算法》：动画图解、一键运行的数据结构与算法教程。支持简中、繁中、English、日本語，提供 Python, Java, C++, C, C#, JS, Go, Swift, Rust, Ruby, Kotlin, TS, Dart 等代码实现
github_trending.summary: This repository provides an animated, runnable tutorial for data structures and algorithms, supporting multiple languages and offering code implementations in over a dozen programming languages.

github_trending.description: freeCodeCamp.org's open-source codebase and curriculum. Learn math, programming, and computer science for free.
github_trending.summary: The freeCodeCamp repository provides the open-source codebase and comprehensive curriculum for learning math, programming, and computer science entirely for free.

github_trending.description: Open-source infrastructure for Computer-Use Agents. Sandboxes, SDKs, and benchmarks to train and evaluate AI agents that can control full desktops (macOS, Linux, Windows).
github_trending.summary: Cua provides open-source infrastructure, including sandboxes, SDKs, and benchmarks, to train and evaluate AI agents capable of controlling full desktop environments across macOS, Linux, and Windows.

github_trending.description: A complete computer science study plan to become a software engineer.
github_trending.summary: This repository provides a comprehensive computer science study plan, structured as a university curriculum, designed to equip individuals with the necessary knowledge for a software engineering career.
